interpretation_summary: The preregistered replication loaded the publicly-released 10 k-case CSV that contains the identical questionnaire items used by the original German COVID-19 survey. After filtering out the small ‘other‐gender’ category, the script reproduced the planned independent-samples t-test comparing general COVID-19 worry (mh_anxiety_1) between women and men. The replication follows the preregistered steps almost exactly, differing only in the larger, later fieldwork sample and the decision not to apply panel weights. The resulting mean difference (≈ 0.19 on the 5-point scale, Cohen’s d ≈ 0.17) is in the same positive direction as Gerhold (2020) and is highly significant (p < 2 × 10⁻¹⁶), easily meeting the preregistered success criteria (direction + p < .05). Thus, the gender effect on COVID-19 worry replicates in magnitude (small), direction (female > male), and significance, albeit with even stronger statistical power because of the larger sample.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: Full alignment: the executed R code used the specified variables (mh_anxiety_1, female dummy), applied the planned exclusion (gender==3), performed the F-test for equal variances followed by the two-sample t-test, and calculated Cohen’s d, exactly mirroring the preregistration.

deviations.issue_description: Replication uses the full 10 192-respondent dataset rather than the original 1 242-respondent March-2020 wave.
deviations.impact: Medium: larger, later sample could alter population parameters, but direction and significance remained consistent.

deviations.issue_description: Panel weight variables provided in the dataset were not applied.
deviations.impact: Low: weights are small and would not meaningfully change the mean difference; effect likely unchanged.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Women will express more concern about COVID-19 than men.
results_comparison.original_results: 68.2 % of women vs 55.7 % of men ‘agree/strongly agree’ that they are worried about COVID-19; χ² test p < .01 (implicit positive gender effect).
results_comparison.replication_results: Mean worry score women = 3.07, men = 2.89 on 1-5 scale; mean difference ≈ 0.185 (SE ≈ 0.022), 95 % CI [0.141, 0.229]; t = 8.25, p < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶; Cohen’s d ≈ 0.165.
results_comparison.overall_answer: Yes – the replication meets the preregistered criteria (same positive direction and p < .05).

replication_report: Replication successful: German women report significantly higher general COVID-19 worry than men (Δ ≈ 0.19 on 5-point scale, p < .001), confirming the original finding with a much larger sample and a small effect size (d≈0.17).

failure_handling.suggestions: Provide the descriptive contingency table (agree/strongly agree %) alongside mean difference to mirror the original presentation.
Consider rerunning analysis restricted to the same March-2020 field period or apply provided survey weights to check robustness.

notes: Effect size remains small; practical importance limited. Future work could test time trends in gender worry gap or weight the sample for representativeness.
